answer:
**Charles Comte's Foundational Claim (Traité de la propriété, Vol. I)**

Comte grounds property in the prior existence of natural liberty, which is the "essential condition of the exercise of every right, and of the accomplishment of every duty."[^1] Liberty consists in the absence of obstacles to a person's "regular development of his being" or "innocent exercise of his faculties," enabling the occupation and appropriation of unowned things.[^2] This extends to territory: nations naturally form via family groupings in basins defined by natural barriers (rivers, mountains), claiming exclusive property over the land sustaining them since time immemorial, guaranteed not by governments but by necessity and mutual defense.[^3] Private property emerges when individuals cultivate national territory, creating value through labor (e.g., clearing, sowing) without usurping others—indeed, multiplying subsistence for all by concentrating production on less land.[^4] Property thus flows directly from liberty's exercise on external things/territory.

**Manu’s Foundational Claim (Manusmṛti, Book 7)**

Manu frames the king's relation to land/kingdom dharmically: the creator endows him with particles from gods (Indra, Sun, etc.) as "protector of this entire world," superior in splendor to mortals, to impose order where chaos reigned.[^5] He wields *Daṇḍa* (punishment/rod), "Dharma itself," to restrain the strong from devouring the weak, ensuring varṇas/āśramas follow their duties; without it, "ownership would not remain with anyone."[^6] The king collects "annual tribute from the kingdom," resides in a well-fortified capital, and acts as "father to his people," his authority divine and imperious yet bound to sacred law (Vedas), humility before Brahmins, and just application of *Daṇḍa*.[^7]

**Differences in Starting Points and Resulting Royal Authority**

Comte starts with universal human liberty as pre-political and natural, yielding property (personal, communal, national) via free labor/occupation—kings/governments emerge later, if at all, merely to protect these prior rights without originating or owning territory.[^8] Manu starts with divine intervention creating the king *ex nihilo* for cosmic Dharma, positioning land/kingdom as his protective domain sustained by tribute and *Daṇḍa*, with subjects' properties/duties subordinate to varṇic order.

Royal authority thus differs sharply: Comte's is *derivative and limited*—a potential agent of national defense or rights-protection, but illegitimate if it violates liberty (e.g., treating people as property); no divine mandate, no inherent tribute-right, as territory is collective/natural, not bestowed.[^9] Manu's is *primary and dharmic*—the king as god-like enforcer of hierarchical order, extracting revenue as duty-fulfiller, his power near-absolute (scorching families like fire) yet checked by Vedas/Brahmins; failure invites destruction, but success yields eternal fame.[^10]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, ch.3, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/03-de-ce-qui-constitue-la-liberte/translation/#p-9b6829).
[^2]: Ibid., [paragraphs](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/03-de-ce-qui-constitue-la-liberte/translation/#p-1c5676).
[^3]: Ibid., ch.7, [paragraphs](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/07-du-territoire-propre-a-chaque-nation/translation/#p-735434) to [p-523da2].
[^4]: Ibid., ch.10, [paragraphs](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/10-de-la-conversion-du-territoire-national-en-proprie/translation/#p-ec2ff8) to [p-30fbf4].
[^5]: Manu, *Manusmṛti*, ch.7, [paragraphs](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-b46e87) to [p-82334c).
[^6]: Ibid., [paragraphs](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-73f910) to [p-32b6e6).
[^7]: Ibid., [paragraph](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-99f678); ch.7 [overview](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/).
[^8]: Comte, ch.3, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/03-de-ce-qui-constitue-la-liberte/translation/#p-86c544).
[^9]: Ibid., ch.7, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/07-du-territoire-propre-a-chaque-nation/translation/#p-982e4a).
[^10]: Manu, [paragraphs](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-9b45ce) to [p-3f0200).
